cases.prompt: package this project so other teams can pip install it
set up pyproject.toml for my library
which build backend should I use, hatchling or uv_build?
build a wheel and check what actually ends up inside it
switch this repo from poetry to uv
modernize this old setup.py project
uv build fails saying the backend can't find my module
shoud my python lib use a src layout?
my wheel doesn't include py.typed even though the file is in the repo
make this a proper installable package with a CLI entry point
convert our requirements.txt workflow to a lockfile-based setup
where do dev dependencies go, dependency-groups or optional-dependencies?
publish this package to PyPI
bump the version and tag a release
set up trusted publishing so CI can upload the package
make this package typed - add py.typed and strict mypy
add [tool.ruff] to pyproject.toml and fix the lint errors
add a GitHub Actions workflow that builds and tests the package
run the test suite on 3.11, 3.12 and 3.13 with uv
audit our dependencies for known vulnerabilities
set up dependabot so uv.lock stays fresh
generate a changelog from conventional commits for the next release
Package this library properly. It currently has a flat mylib/ directory, a setup.py, and a requirements.txt. I want a modern pyproject-based setup managed with uv.
expected_behavior: moves the package to a src/ layout and explains that tests then exercise the installed package instead of the working-directory copy
writes a pyproject.toml with complete [project] metadata (name, version, description, readme, requires-python, license, dependencies)
picks a build backend and states the concrete trade-off driving the choice (pure Python vs C extensions vs VCS-tag versioning)
carries requirements.txt entries into [project.dependencies] / [dependency-groups] before deleting setup.py and requirements.txt
runs uv build and verifies the sdist and wheel contents before declaring the task done

cases.prompt: which build backend should I pick for a new pure-python library?
expected_behavior: compares uv_build, hatchling, and setuptools with concrete trade-offs instead of declaring one absolute winner
shows a [build-system] snippet that pins uv_build with an upper version bound and explains why the bound matters
states that uv_build does not support C or Cython extension modules
does not present unverified speed multipliers as established fact

cases.prompt: my built wheel is missing py.typed even though the file exists in the repo
expected_behavior: inspects the built wheel contents (unzip -l or the bundled check script) to confirm the file is absent from the artifact
identifies which build backend is in use and adds the matching include or package-data configuration
rebuilds and verifies py.typed is now present inside the wheel, not just in the source tree
keeps scope to file inclusion mechanics and defers typing strategy or type-checker setup to the python-typing skill

cases.prompt: migrate this poetry project to uv
expected_behavior: converts [tool.poetry] metadata and dependencies to PEP 621 [project] fields and dev dependencies to [dependency-groups]
switches [build-system] away from poetry-core only after verifying resolution with uv lock and uv sync
commits uv.lock and warns against running pip inside the uv-managed project
runs uv build at the end to prove artifacts still build

cases.prompt: uv build fails: the backend can't find my module
expected_behavior: checks the layout against what uv_build expects (src/<module>/__init__.py or flat <module>/, module named after the normalized project name)
uses [tool.uv.build-backend] module-name or module-root when the import name differs from the project name
mentions that uv_build allows exactly one top-level module per wheel
re-runs uv build to confirm the fix
